top_drugs: RIBOCICLIB SUCCINATE*, TRILACICLIB DIHYDROCHLORIDE*, RIBOCICLIB*, TRILACICLIB*
gbm_note: CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib is CNS-penetrant; palbo/ribo). Rational when CDK4/6-amplified AND RB1 intact AND CDKN2A/B deleted. Among the more defensible live shots.
Cyclin D2 amplification feeds CDK4/6 -- same CDK4/6i rationale (RB1 intact).

top_drugs: EVEROLIMUS*, TRAMETINIB DIMETHYL SULFOXIDE*, VEMURAFENIB*, COBIMETINIB*
gbm_note: Loss activates RAS/MEK; MEK inhibitors (selumetinib/trametinib) have a rationale and real activity in NF1-driven low-grade glioma/pNF -- weaker evidence in GBM.
Same p53-restoration rationale as MDM2 in TP53-WT tumors; fewer clinical agents.
MDM2 inhibitors (milademetan, navtemadlin/AMG-232) restore p53 -- ONLY rational in TP53-wild-type, MDM2-amplified tumors. Early-phase; CNS exposure a question.

Defines IDH-MUTANT glioma (NOT primary IDH-wildtype GBM). Vorasidenib (BBB-penetrant) FDA-approved 2024 for grade-2 IDH-mut glioma (INDIGO) -- a real recent win, but a different disease than your father's primary GBM if it was IDH-wildtype.

top_drugs: CAPMATINIB*, TEPOTINIB*, CAPMATINIB HYDROCHLORIDE*, TEPOTINIB HYDROCHLORIDE*
gbm_note: MET amplification/exon14: crizotinib/capmatinib -- anecdotal/early responses in MET-altered glioma; CNS exposure variable.
FGFR3-TACC3 fusion is the actionable lesion; FGFR inhibitors show subset responses.

top_drugs: DABRAFENIB*, VEMURAFENIB*, ENCORAFENIB*, TRAMETINIB DIMETHYL SULFOXIDE*
gbm_note: BRAF V600E (rare in GBM; epithelioid GBM/pediatric): dabrafenib+trametinib gives REAL responses -- FDA tumor-agnostic approval. A genuine win in the small V600E+ subset.

top_drugs: PALBOCICLIB*, ABEMACICLIB*, RIBOCICLIB*, TRAMETINIB DIMETHYL SULFOXIDE*
gbm_note: Homozygous-deletion is the lesion (no direct drug); sensitizes to CDK4/6i and is a poor-prognosis marker. Target the pathway (CDK4/6), not the gene.
Co-deleted with CDKN2A on 9p21; same CDK4/6-pathway rationale.
Loss -> resistance to CDK4/6i (RB1 must be intact for CDK4/6i to work). A negative selection marker, not a target.
Loss -> alternative-lengthening-of-telomeres phenotype; a marker, not yet a target.
RAS-pathway regulator; loss stabilizes RAS. No direct drug.
RNA-binding tumor suppressor; not druggable.

Most-attacked, least-conquered GBM target. EGFR TKIs (erlotinib/gefitinib/afatinib) failed-poor BBB penetration + heterogeneity. Depatux-m ADC failed ph3 (INTELLANCE); rindopepimut EGFRvIII vaccine failed ph3 (ACT IV). EGFRvIII CAR-T early, antigen escape.
Loss activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR -- not directly druggable; downstream mTOR (everolimus) gave minimal GBM benefit.
Loss-of-function; not directly druggable. (When intact + MDM2-amp, target MDM2.)
Imatinib/dasatinib and multi-TKIs failed in unselected GBM; the PDGFRA-amplified/mutant subset has rarely been tested prospectively with a CNS-active agent.
PI3K-AKT-mTOR axis. Buparlisib failed (poor penetration); paxalisib (BBB-penetrant PI3K/mTOR) is in trials. Pathway redundancy limits single-agent benefit.
Same PI3K axis; regulatory-subunit loss activates signaling. Same caveats as PIK3CA.
MYC-family amplification; 'undruggable' TF -- indirect strategies (BET/Aurora) experimental.
SHP2 (RAS pathway); SHP2 inhibitors experimental, not GBM-validated.
Stemness TF; not directly druggable.
